preFix.title: Spinning from combed top
preFix.subtitle: Using commercial combed top to spin a smooth worsted-style yarn on a drop spindle
preFix.excerpt: Commercial combed top has all fibres aligned in parallel. Spin it with a short forward draw to produce a smooth, dense worsted-style yarn with clear stitch definition.
preFix.sourceNotes: Synthesised from introductory spinning texts including Abby Franquemont 'Respect the Spindle' and general worsted-preparation spinning practice.

content.text: Commercial
combed top

is one of the most consistent fibre preparations available: the fibres are parallel, the width is uniform, and the twist travels predictably along the draft zone. This consistency makes it a good choice for a first spinning project. Variables reduce to drafting angle and twist level, not also fibre preparation. The resulting yarn is worsted-style: smooth, dense, with low halo and clear stitch definition. Compare this with yarn spun from a
rolag
, which is carded rather than combed and spun with a woollen draw to produce a softer, airier fabric.

items.name: Commercial combed top (merino, BFL, or similar)
Leader yarn (20 cm of thin commercial yarn)

content.text: Method
Step 1: Prepare the top

Top is usually sold in a large braided length. Do not pull from the end as it arrives: instead, grip the braid at two points roughly 10 cm apart and pull the hands apart to strip the top lengthwise into a ribbon 2 to 3 cm wide. Continue stripping until you have a ribbon the full length of the braid. Pre-drafting this way removes the step of fighting against the compressed braid while also spinning, which is a common cause of frustration for those new to the craft.
Step 2: Attach the leader
Tie a
leader yarn

around the shaft of the spindle, below the whorl, and pass it up through the hook. Let 15 to 20 cm of leader hang free. Overlap the end of the leader with 5 to 6 cm of the pre-drafted top ribbon. Spin the spindle clockwise (for Z-twist) and the twist will travel into the overlap, joining the top to the leader. Once the join is secure, begin drafting.
Step 3: Draft with a short forward draw

Hold the fibre supply in the back hand. Place the forward hand close to the hook, pinching the yarn just below the twist-front. With the back hand, draw the fibre supply back 3 to 5 cm to attenuate the ribbon. Release the pinch of the forward hand and allow twist to run into the drafted section. The
short forward draw
keeps the fibres parallel throughout the process and compresses them into a smooth, round yarn. Spin the spindle again when it slows, before twist runs out and the single loses cohesion.
Step 4: Wind onto the cop
When 20 to 25 cm of spun yarn accumulates below the hook, unhook the yarn and wind it around the shaft just below the whorl to build the
cop

. Wind at a slight angle, building a conical shape that keeps the cop stable. Re-hook and continue spinning. Check the twist level by allowing a short section to double back on itself: a balanced single for weaving plies evenly, while a single intended for plying should have a visible over-twist that the plying step will release.
Step 5: Wind off and finish the skein

When the cop is full or the top is used up, wind the yarn onto the niddy-noddy in figure-8 circuits. Tie in four places with waste cotton. Soak in 30 °C water for 15 minutes, squeeze gently, and hang to dry. The twist will relax slightly as the yarn blooms, giving the finished skein a rounder, softer profile than the freshly spun single.

items.fix: Keep a firm pinch directly at the twist-front. Do not allow the twist to pass the forward hand until you have drawn the fibre back first.
items.cause: Pinch on the forward hand too relaxed
items.problem: Twist runs into the fibre supply before drafting

items.fix: Spin the spindle more before releasing. The draft section should feel firm and resist being pulled apart before you allow the spindle to hang.
items.cause: Insufficient twist in the drafted section
items.problem: Single breaks when the spindle is released

items.fix: Slow down and draw only 3 cm per draft. Pre-draft the ribbon thinner before starting: it should be nearly translucent when held to a window.
items.cause: Draft zone inconsistent; back hand pulling uneven amounts

items.fix: Build the base of the cop with 10 to 12 horizontal wraps around the shaft before adding angled wraps. This gives the cop a seat to rest on.
items.cause: Insufficient initial wraps or winding angle too steep
